coredata.dc:title: A lantern-shaped fluorescent probe based on viologen/polyoxometalate for the detection of Ag+ in beverages and daily necessities

coredata.dc:description: A lantern-shaped viologen/polyoxometalate (POM)-based compound [NiII(MSBP)2(H2O)2]·(β-Mo8O26)·H2O (Ni-POM) (MSBP = 1-(4-Methanesulfonyl-benzyl)-[4,4']bipyridinyl-1-ium) was successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method for the efficient detection of Ag+. A strong affinity between Ag+ and SO in the viologen component of the Ni-POM structure made them interact, which led to blue fluorescence quenching. In the concentration range of 0.1–4 μM, a strong linear relationship was observed between the Ag+concentration and the fluorescence intensity ratio of Ni-POM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 20.4 nM. Considering the widespread presence of Ag+ in various water sources, daily necessities and food preservatives, the utilization of Ni-POM for detecting the concentration of Ag+ in real samples (water, daily necessities and beverages) was proved to be highly effective. Moreover, a remarkable recovery rate ranging from 95.70 % to 103.60 % was achieved, indicating that the monitoring results of practical samples were satisfactory. A fluorescent ink based on Ni-POM was designed for the purpose of information confidentiality. More importantly, the hydrogel intelligent device for visual detection of Ag+ was developed, which could realize visual real-time on-site quantitative detection of Ag+ concentration in beverages and daily necessities. Therefore, Ni-POM provides an effective platform for the development of visually quantitative detection of Ag+ in food and daily necessities.

head.ce:title: A lantern-shaped fluorescent probe based on viologen/polyoxometalate for the detection of Ag+ in beverages and daily necessities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A lantern-shaped viologen/polyoxometalate (POM)-based compound [NiII(MSBP)2(H2O)2]·(β-Mo8O26)·H2O (Ni-POM) (MSBP = 1-(4-Methanesulfonyl-benzyl)-[4,4']bipyridinyl-1-ium) was successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method for the efficient detection of Ag+. A strong affinity between Ag+ and SO in the viologen component of the Ni-POM structure made them interact, which led to blue fluorescence quenching. In the concentration range of 0.1–4 μM, a strong linear relationship was observed between the Ag+concentration and the fluorescence intensity ratio of Ni-POM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 20.4 nM. Considering the widespread presence of Ag+ in various water sources, daily necessities and food preservatives, the utilization of Ni-POM for detecting the concentration of Ag+ in real samples (water, daily necessities and beverages) was proved to be highly effective. Moreover, a remarkable recovery rate ranging from 95.70 % to 103.60 % was achieved, indicating that the monitoring results of practical samples were satisfactory. A fluorescent ink based on Ni-POM was designed for the purpose of information confidentiality. More importantly, the hydrogel intelligent device for visual detection of Ag+ was developed, which could realize visual real-time on-site quantitative detection of Ag+ concentration in beverages and daily necessities. Therefore, Ni-POM provides an effective platform for the development of visually quantitative detection of Ag+ in food and daily necessities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the rapid development of modern industry and the widespread use of various products have raised significant concerns regarding the quality and safety of consumer goods, especially food preservatives and daily necessities [1,2]. Silver ion (Ag+) has become a significant target among various heavy metal ions families due to its effective tissue fusion, antimicrobial capabilities and corrosion resistance, which covers wide-ranging applications in chemical engineering, the food industry and biopharmaceuticals [3–6]. However, despite its potential advantages, the potential toxicity of Ag+ cannot be overlooked. The release of accumulated Ag+ into the environment can have negative impacts on aquatic organisms and ecosystems. It can disrupt the growth, reproduction, and survival abilities of aquatic organisms [7–10]. Ag+ can enter the human body through various routes, including inhalation, ingestion or contact with the skin [11–14]. Long-term exposure to high levels of Ag+ is associated with cellular toxicity, genetic toxicity and oxidative stress, which may lead to adverse health effects [15,16]. Consequently, accurate detection of Ag+ concentrations in real samples is an urgent requirement in the field of life sciences. The detection of Ag+ is crucial for maintaining safe levels of Ag+ in drinking water, food and the environment, ensuring human health [17]. Furthermore, monitoring and regulating Ag+ release is vital for preserving water ecosystems and ecological balance. People can better understand the extent of Ag+ contamination in the environment by detecting Ag+ concentrations, allowing for effective remediation and protective measures [18].

With the progress of science and technology, instrumental analysis methods are gradually applied to the detection of heavy metal ions. For example, atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry and other instrumental analysis methods can provide more accurate measurement results of heavy metal ion concentration [19–22]. However, these traditional instrumental analysis methods have some limitations in real-time monitoring, simple operation and low cost. Therefore, in recent years, fluorescent probe detection has become one of the most favorable methods for heavy metal ions detection due to its high sensitivity, high selectivity and real-time monitoring, and has been widely concerned and applied [23,24]. The fluorescent signal is generated by the coordination reaction or energy transfer between specific fluorescent probe molecules and heavy metal ions in the fluorescent probe detection method. These fluorescent probes have high selectivity and sensitivity, and can detect specific heavy metal ions quickly and accurately [25,26]. At the same time, the fluorescent probe has the ability of real-time monitoring and the detection results can be obtained in a short time. The selective detection of different heavy metal ions can be realized by adjusting the structure of fluorescent probe, which is expected to be widely used in environmental monitoring, food safety and other fields [27,28].

Viologens are organic compounds that contain a cationic structure, typically consisting of two connected pyridinium rings. Viologens can actually be utilized as a photochromic material for metal ions detection, which exhibit reversible discoloration upon exposure to light or other stimuli [29,30]. This unique characteristic has led to its wide application in various fields, including smart windows, information storage, energy conversion and inkless printing [31–33]. According to the discoloration effect of viologens when encountering stimulation, it can become an excellent visual fluorescent probe. The presence or concentration of target ions can be identified by visual observation of light-induced discoloration or changes in fluorescence response, which is the direct result of the combination of target ions, real-time and quantitative monitoring can be realized [34,35]. In addition, the synthesis steps of the viologen structure are relatively simple, so it can adapt to different ion detection applications, making it a multifunctional tool in the field of chemical sensing.

Polyoxometalates (POMs) have special molecular structures and electronic energy level distributions, which enable them to generate fluorescence signals under excitation light [36,37]. POMs can be used to design fluorescent probes to detect fluorescent signals with different wavelengths, which improves the selectivity and detection ability of different ions [38,39]. POMs have high thermal stability and chemical stability, and can keep their fluorescence characteristics under different environmental conditions, which makes the POM-based fluorescent probe have a long service life and the excellent capacity of anti-interference [40,41]. The structure of polyoxometalates can be regulated by adjusting the combination of metal ions, the selection and modification of ligands, making the POM-based fluorescent probe more customizable, and can adjust the selectivity and sensitivity of different ions to meet specific detection requirements [42,43]. Viologen-based fluorescent probes exhibit visible color changes upon exposure to intense light stimulation. However, a drawback of these probes is the decrease in fluorescence intensity over time when exposed to prolonged light. In order to overcome this limitation, the introduction of robust and thermally stable POMs into the probe's structure can provide protection to the viologen moiety, preventing photobleaching and thereby ensuring a prolonged lifespan and optimal performance.

In this work, a lantern-shaped viologen/POM-based compound [NiII(MSBP)2(H2O)2]·(β-Mo8O26)·H2O (Ni-POM) (MSBP = 1-(4-Methanesulfonyl-benzyl)-[4,4']bipyridinyl-1-ium) was successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method and further to construct an intelligent sensing device of solid hydrogel. Due to the introduction of viologen ligand and POM in the structure of Ni-POM, it exhibited good sensitivity, selectivity, optical stability and biocompatibility. There was a strong affinity between SO on the surface of Ni-POM and Ag+, and its good properties could also be used as a promising fluorescent probe to detect Ag+ by fluorescence "on-off" signal response. Moreover, a Ni-POM-based fluorescent ink was designed, which could be used for information confidentiality. The hydrogel intelligent device for visually detecting trace Ag+ in daily necessities and beverages was further developed by analyzing RGB and HSV with Adobe Color software. It provides a potential method and is expected to be widely used in the fields of environmental monitoring and food safety. The detailed experimental process was shown in Scheme 1 .

Acrylamide (AAM), ammonium persulphate, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and 1-(4-Methanesulfonyl-benzyl)-[4,4']bipyridinyl-1-ium (HMSBP) were provided by Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. Silver nitrate and other metal salts were all purchased from Kelong Reagent Co., Ltd. All organic reagents were obtained from RunZe Reagent Co., Ltd.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was conducted using an Ultima IV X-ray powder diffractometer (Kurary, Tokyo, Japan) operating at 40 kV and 40 mA with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the synthesized products were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer and a PerkinElmer luminescence spectrometer (LS-55), respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed to identify the functional groups present in the products using a Shimadzu IR Affinity 1S spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the elemental composition of the samples using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Single crystal X-ray diffraction data was collected on a Bruker APEX-IV CCD diffractometer with graphite monochromatic Mo Kα radiation at 298 K. The crystal structure was solved by direct methods and refined using the full matrix least-squares method on F
2 embedded in the SHELXTL program, with Olex2 serving as the graphical interface. The crystallographic data for this work can be found in the Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre (CCDC) with the deposition number CCDC 2292861.

A mixture of 0.05 mmol (17.3 mg) of HMSBP, 0.15 mmol (26.7 mg) of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, 0.15 mmol (35.7 mg) of NiCl2·6H2O and 15 mL deionized water in a closed 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave (pH = 3.5 by HCl) was heated at 130 °C for 7 days and then cooled to room temperature naturally. Blue block-shaped crystals were collected by filtration (yield: 66 %, based on Mo). Calculated for C36H44Mo8N4NiO35S2: C 21.81, H 2.24, N 2.83 %. Found: C 21.92, H 2.26, N 2.89. IR (KBr, cm−1): ν 3331, 3211, 2995, 2912, 1631, 1546, 1305, 1205, 1145, 931, 669.

All fluorescence tests were carried out in PBS buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.12 mM (0.25 mg/mL) Ni-POM powders. In order to study the selectivity and anti-interference ability of Ni-POM in detecting Ag+, the fluorescence spectra of Ni-POM and the mixture of interfering ions in the presence and absence of Ag+ were studied. 10 μM of various cations (Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Sn4+) were mixed with PBS buffer solution of Ni-POM and reacted at room temperature for 30 s. Then, 1 μM Ag+ was added into the test tube one by one, which was acted again for 30 s at room temperature. To ensure the stability and reliability of the experimental results, three fluorescence intensities were recorded and averaged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of environmental water, daily necessities and beverages
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water from Bohai University Laboratory, lake water from Artificial Lake of Bohai University and Orange juice, Tomato juice, Grape juice, Apple juice, Astringent, Disinfection spray, Mouthwash, Shampoo purchased from local supermarket. The actual samples of daily necessities purchased were put into a beaker and diluted by distilled water 100 times. The samples were heated for 30 min, then cooled to room temperature. To improve the accuracy of the test results, it is necessary to address the presence of insoluble solid residues in beverages and daily commodities purchased from stores. Initially, an appropriate amount of the actual sample solution is heated to dissolve the solid residues as much as possible, ensuring that the solution reaches a saturated state. Subsequently, filtration is employed to remove the insoluble solid residues that were not dissolved during the heating process. Finally, the practical samples were mixed with PBS buffer solution. In order to determine the enhanced recovery rate of the constructed fluorescent probe, 50, 100, 300 and 460 nM Ag+ solutions were added to the standard solution of the practical samples.

A dispersion of 0.0015 mmol (3 mg) of Ni-POM was created in a mixed solvent consisting of 30 mL of H2O and 5 mL of ethanol. This specific type of ink was suitable for handwriting purposes, which could be utilized for anti-counterfeiting operations.

To prepare Ni-POM-based fluorescent hydrogel, the following steps were executed: 0.076 mmol (0.15 g) of Ni-POM was introduced into 20 mL of distilled water and mixed thoroughly. Afterward, a total of 28 mmol (2.00 g) of AAM and 0.44 mmol (0.10 g) of ammonium persulfate were added to the solution. To ensure optimal dispersion, the mixture underwent ultrasonic treatment in an ice water bath for a period of 2 min. To eliminate any bubbles, the mixture was poured into a hydrogel mold and sonicated for an additional 2 min. The reaction took place at 65 °C in an oven, resulting in the formation of Ni-POM-based fluorescent hydrogel. For the visual quantitative testing of the hydrogel, the prepared hydrogels were immersed in solutions containing different concentrations of Ag+ for a period of 2 min. The color response of the hydrogel was recorded by a smartphone camera under both ambient light and 365 nm UV light conditions.

Single crystal X-ray diffraction showed that Ni-POM was crystallized in the monoclinic system and the space group is C2/c. An asymmetric unit of Ni-POM was composed of one Ni2+ ion, two MSBP + ligands, four coordinating H2O molecules and one dissociative [β-Mo8O26]4− polyoxoanion. [β-Mo8O26]4− isomer has a C
2h symmetry, which was constructed by eight {MoO6} octahedra through co-edge method (Fig. 1 a). The bond lengths of Mo–O ranged from 1.6908(18) to 2.4239(16) Å in [β-Mo8O26]4- polyoxoanion (Fig. 1b). The Ni2+ ion was in a six-coordinated environment by four O atoms from four coordination H2O and two N atoms from two MSBP + ligands. Namely, the Ni1 was fused by two MSBP + ligands to construct a [NiII(MSBP)2]4+ subunit. The bond lengths of Ni–O and Ni–N were within the range of 2.020(3)−2.0752(19) and 2.107(2) Å, respectively. The angles of O–Ni–O/N were from 88.11(6) to 179.06(12) Å and the N1–Ni–N1 bond angle was 176.23(13) Å. Furthermore, the following hydrogen bond interactions existed in Ni-POM: O1W–H⋯O5(2.663 Å) and O2W–H⋯O7(2.878 Å). The two-dimensional (2D) hydrogen bond network was formed by the hydrogen bond between [β-Mo8O26]4− polyoxoanion and coordination water molecules in the [NiII(MSBP)2]4+. The 2D hydrogen bond network formed with the ac axis as the plane was shown in Fig. 1c and it presented a novel lantern-shaped in the 2D plane with the ab axis as the plane (Fig. 1d). The details of other specific crystal databases were recorded and are presented in Table S1−S3.

Ni-POM was analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) to evaluate its phase purity (Fig. 2 a). The observed diffraction peaks of the synthesized Ni-POM were closely corresponded with the simulated peaks obtained from the single crystal analysis. This indicates that Ni-POM exhibits excellent phase purity and crystallinity. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was employed to identify and characterize the composition of the specific chemical bonds and functional groups in Ni-POM (Fig. 2b). The strong broad peak observed at 2995 cm−1 could be attributed to the overlapping of the stretching vibration of C–H [44]. The peak observed at 1631 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC/CN, while the peak at 1145 and 1305 cm−1 corresponds to the SO stretching vibrations [45]. Furthermore, the absorption peaks at 669−931 cm−1 were attributed to the presence of [β-Mo8O26]4− in Ni-POM [46].

In view of the excellent optical characteristics and stability of a viologen/POM structure in Ni-POM, it was worthwhile to conduct in-depth research on its optical properties by using UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. When the excitation wavelength was 395 nm, the optimal emission peak of Ni-POM was observed at 455 nm. In the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 2c), the observed absorption band at 275 nm could be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 hybridization of aromatic compounds [47]. Additionally, the peak at 330 nm was associated with the n-π* transitions of the viologen functional groups of SO and 4, 4′-bipyridine [48]. The aqueous solution of Ni-POM exhibited intense blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2c inset). Notably, the relationship between the emission wavelength and the excitation wavelength of Ni-POM was shown in Fig. 2d. The emission wavelength remained unchanged even when the excitation wavelength was varied between 340 nm and 400 nm. This noteworthy observation confirms that the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of Ni-POM remains constant regardless of the excitation wavelength.

Fluorescence stability is a crucial characteristic for an effective fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 2e, the fluorescence intensity of Ni-POM exhibited minimal variation in the range of pH = 4−9, indicating the structural stability of Ni-POM in weakly acidic and neutral environments. Additionally, Ni-POM demonstrated exceptional stability over a period of 60 days with no significant changes in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2f). This finding substantiates Ni-POM as a desirable fluorescent probe exhibiting long-term stability capabilities. Moreover, biocompatibility is another critical factor to consider. In order to replicate the internal environment of living things, NaCl solutions with varying concentrations (from 0 to 2.5 mol L−1) were utilized. Remarkably, the fluorescence intensity of Ni-POM remained relatively stable under these experimental conditions (Fig. S1). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of Ni-POM remained unaffected within the temperature range of 15–60 °C (Fig. S2). It is widely recognized that viologen ligands possess strong photobleaching ability, which is not advantageous for fluorescent probes. Hence, the incorporation of POMs into the crystal structure has garnered considerable attention in research [49]. The photobleaching performance was assessed using a 365 nm UV light. The experimental findings unequivocally demonstrated that Ni-POM maintains a robust fluorescence intensity for a duration of 12 min without experiencing quenching (Fig. S3). This clearly indicates that the modification with POMs effectively mitigates photobleaching, thereby facilitating the reliable utilization of the fluorescent probe in intricate environments. These results collectively demonstrate the exceptional optical stability of Ni-POM, affirming its potential as a fluorescent probe.

To achieve optimal optical sensing performance for detecting Ag+, various detection parameters, including temperature, pH and incubation time of the reaction system, were systematically optimized. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the quenching efficiency reached the maximum at pH = 7. Moreover, considering the excellent fluorescence stability of Ni-POM within the range of pH = 2–9, pH = 7 was chosen as the optimal condition for detecting Ag+. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of the system remains stable even at a high temperature of 60 °C, indicating that the sensing system had remarkable stability at high temperature. In order to ensure experimental convenience, the optimal temperature of 25 °C was chosen for conducting the experiment. (Fig. S4), Additionally, the incubation time between Ni-POM and the target Ag+ was also investigated. The results in Fig. S5 revealed that the response of Ag+to Ni-POM tended to stabilize after 30 s. These optimized experimental conditions, including a stable response at 25 °C, maximum quenching efficiency at pH = 7 and the incubation time of 30 s, are highly advantageous for practical on-the-spot detection.

Based on the optimized detection parameters, the feasibility of the Ni-POM fluorescent probe for Ag+detection was further evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3b, the blue fluorescence intensity of Ni-POM exhibited a gradual decrease with the addition of Ag+. The quantitative analysis of F/F0 (where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the detection system at 455 nm in the absence and presence of Ag+, respectively) and the concentration of Ag+ was presented in Fig. 3c, demonstrating an effective fluorescence quenching of Ni-POM within the Ag + concentrations range of 0.1–5 μM. The relationship between Ag+ concentration and the fluorescent signal ratio (F/F0), revealing a good linear correlation within the range of 0.1–4 μM (R2 = 0.9987). Furthermore, the LOD of Ag+ was determined to be 20.4 nM (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ represented the standard deviation of the blank signals, and k was the slope of the linear calibration curve). This LOD value was lower than the allowable concentration of Ag+ in potable water (460 nM) specified by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). In comparison to other reported methods for Ag+ detection, the proposed method exhibited a significantly lower LOD value (Table S4).

The detection selectivity of the fluorescent probe was further assessed by evaluating its response in the presence of potential co-existing ionic interferents, including the absence and presence of Ag+. As shown in Fig. 3d, when a total of 19 different substances (Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Sn4+) were added to the aqueous solution of Ni-POM, it was observed that the fluorescence of Ni-POM is significantly quenched only in the presence of the target Ag+. Following the introduction of Ag + into the solution containing various interfering ions, a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of all cationic solutions was observed, exhibiting the outstanding anti-interference capability of Ni-POM. The results indicated that Ni-POM exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity in quenching fluorescence in the presence of the target Ag+, while also demonstrating remarkable anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism for detecting Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching phenomenon and explore the practical applications of Ni-POM, various characterization techniques were employed, including PXRD, FT-IR, fluorescence lifetimes, UV–vis and XPS spectra analysis. First of all, the fluorescence lifetime of Ni-POM experienced a significant decrease from 7.25 ns to 2.17 ns while the concentration of Ag+increased, suggesting the occurrence of a dynamic quenching process (Fig. 4 a) [50]. It is worth noting that the alteration in fluorescence lifetime mainly originated from the fast component of the surface viologen functional groups. The high quenching constant (Ksv = 4.3 × 105 M−1) obtained from Fig. S6 suggested a strong interaction between Ni-POM and Ag+. Then, the PXRD analysis of Ni-POM revealed a change in the diffraction pattern with the addition of Ag+ (Fig. 2a). The coordination of Ag+ with the functional groups led to alterations in the crystal structure, resulting in a modification of the diffraction pattern observed in PXRD measurements [51]. Additionally, the UV–vis spectra demonstrated significant changes in the conjugated system of the molecule upon interaction with Ag+, leading to a noticeable redshift in the maximum absorption peak and confirming coordination between Ag+ and Ni-POM (Fig. 4b) [52]. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2b), the stretching vibrations of SO at 1145 and 1305 cm−1 were significantly decreased and a new peak at 592 cm−1 representing Ag–O emerged, supporting the coordination of Ag+ with oxygen-containing functional groups and the disruption of the conjugated aromatic ring [53,54]. The XPS spectra analysis further supported these findings. The presence of Ag3d in the spectrum of Ni-POM + Ag+ was attributed to Ag3d3/2 (373.3 eV) and Ag3d5/2 (367.2 eV) (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the O1s peak in the full spectrum displayed a considerable decrease in intensity (Fig. S7). Similarly, the high-resolution O1s peak also exhibited a redshift in its peak position from 530.9 eV to 531.8 eV, accompanied by a decrease of 65 % in intensity (Fig. S8). Moreover, a new peak corresponding to Ag–O was observed and fitted at 530.2 eV (Fig. 4d) [55]. The strong binding constant (Ka = 3.67 × 104 M−1) between Ni-POM and Ag+ further supported the proposed mechanism (Fig. S9). In summary, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Ni-POM can be attributed to the coordination interaction between Ag+ and the surface viologen functional groups (the SO groups).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of Ag+ in water samples, Beverage and daily necessities
ce:section.ce:para:
Ag+ is commonly found in aquatic products, daily necessities and food preservatives due to their disinfection and anticorrosion properties. However, excessive Ag+ exposure can cause significant health risks. Therefore, the accurate detection of Ag+ in practical samples plays a crucial role in ensuring human safety. To evaluate the reliability of the developed Ni-POM in detecting Ag+ in practical samples, Ni-POM was employed in water samples (Tap water from Bohai University Laboratory and lake water from Artificial Lake of Bohai University), daily necessities (Astringent, Disinfection spray, Mouthwash and Shampoo) and beverages (Orange juice, Tomato juice, Grape juice and Apple juice), and Ag+ with concentrations of 50, 100, 300 and 460 nM were introduced. The recovery rates of Ag+ in water samples, beverages and daily necessities were 99.65–103.55 %, 96.60−101.60 % and 95.70−98.95 %, respectively (Table S5, Tables 1 and 2
). Moreover, the percentage standard deviation (%, RSD) of all practical samples were less than 4.00 %. These excellent detection performances indicated that Ni-POM could reliably and accurately detect Ag+ in practical samples (including water samples, daily necessities and food products), contributing to the advancement of research in areas related to human health and safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Ni-POM information confidentiality technology
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the information confidentiality level, a complex encryption and decryption process was devised, taking advantage of the high sensitivity of fluorescence color and intensity of Ni-POM towards Ag+. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a fluorescent ink was formulated by incorporating Ni-POM and the mixed solution of ethanol and water, which was subsequently added to the pre-prepared refills. Then, the designated pattern "888″ was written on the filter paper using this fluorescent ink. Initially, the pattern remained invisible under ambient light. However, when exposed to 365 nm UV light, the first layer of information "888″ became distinctly visible. Subsequently, the previously prepared Ag+ solution was applied to the filter paper. After 365 nm UV light was switched off, a camouflage information layer in the configuration of "111″ was generated. The second layer of information "111″ became visible under ambient light, while the information "333″ remained concealed. Finally, the "secret" information encoded as "333″ was unlocked and displayed by switching on 365 nm UV light (Fig. 5b and c). This process incorporates various external chemical and light stimuli to obtain different types of information, which demonstrates that misleading information can be written to ensure that only authorized individuals gain access to the correct information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual quantitative detection of Ag+ by hydrogel intelligent device
ce:section.ce:para: In order to facilitate the intuitive and simple analysis of the analytes, a hydrogel intelligent device for the visual detection of Ag+ was developed. The hydrogel intelligent device provided a more convenient, time-saving and cost-effective method for quantitatively detecting Ag+ by naked eyes. The blank hydrogel had no inherent fluorescence or color under ambient light. However, when Ni-POM was incorporated into the blank hydrogel, a striking change in its optical properties was observed. Ni-POM + hydrogel exhibited intense blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, which provided a distinct and easily recognizable visual indication of the presence of Ni-POM (Figs. 6a and S10). Furthermore, to demonstrate the capability of the Ni-POM + hydrogel to effectively trace Ag+, the hydrogel was immersed in an aqueous solution containing Ag+. The results showed that the fluorescence of Ni-POM + hydrogel was decreased and its discoloration from colorless to brown under ambient light, which provided a visual indication of the presence of Ag+ (Fig. 6 b). The decrease in fluorescence and accompanying discoloration directly confirmed the interaction between hydrogel and Ag+.

Firstly, Ni-POM + hydrogel was exposed to different concentrations of Ag + solutions ranging from 0 to 500 nM. Afterward, Ni-POM + hydrogel was kept in a dark environment to minimize interference from ambient light. Images of the hydrogel were captured by a smartphone under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and ambient light. The captured images were processed using Adobe Color software to obtain the RGB and HSV values, which facilitated easier color comparison and analysis. The relationship between the Ag+ concentrations and the R/B values of the hydrogel intelligent device was found to be linear within the concentration range of 0–500 nM (y = 0.00585x + 1.0002, R2 = 0.9968) (Fig. 6c). The LOD for Ag+ was determined to be 17.7 nM, which was a highly satisfactory result and provided sufficient demand for the extraction and detection of Ag+ in daily life and environmental applications. Similarly, the H/V values of the hydrogel intelligent device also exhibited a linear relationship with the Ag+ concentration within the range of 0–500 nM (y = 0.0071x + 0.0018, R2 = 0.9994) (Fig. S11). And the LOD in this case was 17.5 nM. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel intelligent device containing Ni-POM was monitored for up to 60 days, demonstrating good storage stability (Figs. 6d and S12). The results confirmed that the smartphone-assisted hydrogel intelligent device maintained its performance over an extended period, enabling rapid and accurate measurements of the target Ag+.

In order to assess the practicality of Ni-POM + hydrogel for the target analyte, selective identification and anti-interference performance were evaluated. The aforementioned interfering substances were incorporated into the hydrogel to simulate the practical conditions. Figs. 6e and S13 illustrated the discoloration when Ag+ and common interfering substances were added to Ni-POM + hydrogel. It was observed that only Ag + caused a significant response, resulting in a distinct intensification of the visible color and a decrease in fluorescence were observed. The presence of interfering substances had minimal influence on the discoloration of the hydrogel system, suggesting the high selectivity of the proposed hydrogel for Ag+. Moreover, significant discolorations were readily observable to the naked eye when Ag+ and co-existing interfering substances were co-incubated. (Figs. 6f and S14). Remarkably, it was worth noting that the response of Ni-POM + hydrogel to Ag+ was consistent under common interference, which further highlighted its excellent anti-interference ability. Additionally, the hydrogel itself acted as a barrier, effectively impeding the infiltration of biomacromolecules and small molecules through its porous structure, which further enhanced the anti-interference ability and selectivity of the hydrogel system [56]. In summary, the hydrogel intelligent device of Ni-POM + hydrogel provided the advantage of visual monitoring and semi-quantitative analysis of Ag+ by only naked eyes. Ni-POM + hydrogel demonstrates excellent selectivity and anti-interference capacity, which makes it a practical and reliable tool for the detection and analysis of Ag+ in various application scenarios.

Based on the results obtained from the above research, Ni-POM + hydrogel was placed in Petri dishes in order to verify the detection ability of Ag+ in the practical samples. Ag+ with concentrations of 100, 460 or 50, 500 nM were added to beverages and daily necessities, respectively. The color of the hydrogel changed from colorless to brown-black under ambient light, while it was accompanied by a noticeable quenching of the strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Furthermore, the temperature resistance of Ni-POM + hydrogel was studied to evaluate its stability. The color of the hydrogel remained unchanged after being stored at temperatures of 4 °C and 25 °C for one month (Fig. 7 and Fig. S15). This observation indicated that the Ni-POM + hydrogel exhibited good potential for convenient and quantitative detection of Ag+ in beverages and daily necessities, even under varying temperature conditions. It demonstrates excellent potential for the rapid, reliable and quantitative detection of Ag+ in various consumer products, facilitating the monitoring and maintenance of Ag+ concentrations to ensure the safety of humankind.

In summary, a lantern-shaped viologen/POM-based compound [NiII(MSBP)2(H2O)2]·(β-Mo8O26)·H2O (Ni-POM) for Ag+ detection in beverages and daily necessities was successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Ni-POM exhibited excellent selectivity for detecting Ag+, with a wide response range of 0.1–4 μM and the LOD is 20.4 nM. This fluorescent probe demonstrated its potential for quantitative determination of Ag+ concentration in environmental water samples, beverages and daily necessities, which had high accuracy and satisfactory recovery rates. According to the fluorescence quenching effect caused by the interaction between Ni-POM and Ag+, a fluorescent ink was developed, which played a crucial role in ensuring the confidentiality of information. In order to further improve the practicability and convenience of Ag+ detection, a hydrogel intelligent device was developed. This device incorporated both the visual discoloration of the hydrogel and the smartphone analysis of colors RGB and HSV by Adobe software, allowing for on-the-spot detection of Ag+. The hydrogel intelligent device offered remarkable portability, cost-effectiveness, ease of operation, rapid response and vivid color display, which made it highly suitable for convenient on-site visual detection and accurate monitoring of Ag+ in food and daily necessity samples.

Kai Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. Tao Liu: Formal analysis. Jun Ying: Validation, Investigation. Aixiang Tian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xiuli Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.
